title: Color Spaces: RGB, CMY, HSL, YUV, YCbCr

content.body:
**Additive color (RGB):** Mixing light. Used in screens/projectors. Start from black вАФ adding light adds color.
- R+G = Yellow
- R+B = Magenta
- G+B = Cyan
- R+G+B = White

**Subtractive color (CMY):** Mixing ink/pigments. Used in printing. Start from white paper вАФ adding ink removes color.
- C+M = Blue, C+Y = Green, M+Y = Red
- C+M+Y = Black (in theory вАФ in practice printers add real Black ink = CMYK)

Cyan, Magenta, Yellow. The inverse of RGB:
$$C = 1-R, \quad M = 1-G, \quad Y_c = 1-B \quad (R,G,B \in [0,1])$$
Cyan ink **absorbs red**, Magenta ink **absorbs green**, Yellow ink **absorbs blue**.

content.title: CMY Worked Examples
content.body:
**Pure Red** (R=1, G=0, B=0): C=0, M=1, Y=1
Magenta ink + Yellow ink = removes green and blue вЖТ only red shows. вЬУ

**Teal** (R=0, G=0.5, B=1): C=1, M=0.5, Y=0
Full cyan, half magenta, no yellow.

**White** (R=1, G=1, B=1): C=0, M=0, Y=0
No ink at all вЖТ white paper shows through. вЬУ

**Black** (R=0, G=0, B=0): C=1, M=1, Y=1
Maximum all three inks вЖТ black. (Real printers add K=black ink for better quality = CMYK) вЬУ

More **intuitive** for humans than RGB:

**H = Hue** (color type, angle 0¬∞вАУ360¬∞ on color wheel):
- 0¬∞ = Red
- 60¬∞ = Yellow
- 120¬∞ = Green
- 180¬∞ = Cyan
- 240¬∞ = Blue
- 300¬∞ = Magenta
- 360¬∞ = Red again

**S = Saturation:** Vividness. High = vivid color. Low = grayish. Zero = pure gray.

**L/V/I = Lightness/Value/Intensity:** Brightness. Different names = slightly different formulas, same concept.

Separates brightness from color. Why? **Backwards compatibility** (B&W TVs show Y only) and **compression** (eye is less sensitive to color than brightness).

$$Y = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$$
$$U = B - Y \quad (\text{blue difference})$$
$$V = R - Y \quad (\text{red difference})$$

- Green gets weight **0.587** (highest) вАФ eye most sensitive to green
- Blue gets weight **0.114** (lowest) вАФ eye least sensitive to blue
- Weights sum to 1.0 вЖТ for gray (R=G=B=k), Y=k exactly
- For gray pixels: U=V=**0** (no color offset)

content.title: YUV вАФ Four Worked Examples
content.body:
**Example 1 вАФ Reddish color** R=200, G=100, B=50:
Y = 0.299√Ч200 + 0.587√Ч100 + 0.114√Ч50 = 59.8 + 58.7 + 5.7 = **124.2**
U = 50 вИТ 124.2 = **вИТ74.2** (less blue than average)
V = 200 вИТ 124.2 = **75.8** (more red than average) вЬУ

**Example 2 вАФ Gray** R=G=B=150:
Y = 150 √Ч (0.299+0.587+0.114) = 150 √Ч 1.0 = **150**
U = 0, V = 0 (no color) вЬУ

**Example 3 вАФ Pure blue** R=0, G=0, B=255:
Y = 0.114√Ч255 = **29.07** вЖР **very dark!** Blue looks dim to the eye
U = 255 вИТ 29.07 = **225.93** (lots of blue)
V = 0 вИТ 29.07 = **вИТ29.07** (no red)

**Example 4 вАФ Pure green** R=0, G=255, B=0:
Y = 0.587√Ч255 = **149.685** вЖР much brighter than blue!
U = 0 вИТ 149.685 = **вИТ149.685**
V = 0 вИТ 149.685 = **вИТ149.685**

Scaled version of YUV used in **JPEG** and **MPEG**. R,G,B in [0,1]:
$$Y = 65.481R + 128.553G + 24.966B + 16$$
$$C_b = -37.797R - 74.203G + 112.000B + 128$$
$$C_r = 112.000R - 93.786G - 18.214B + 128$$

**Ranges:**
- Y: **16 (black) to 235 (white)** вАФ NOT 0 and 255!
- Cb, Cr: centered at **128** (=no color offset). Above 128 = more, below = less.
- For gray (R=G=B): Cb=128, Cr=128 exactly.

**Why JPEG converts to YCbCr first:** The eye is much more sensitive to brightness (Y) than color (Cb, Cr). JPEG can reduce Cb and Cr resolution by **half in each direction** (chroma subsampling) without people noticing. This is a major reason for JPEG's high compression ratios.

**Color model summary:**
- RGB: displays/cameras (additive)
- CMY: printing (subtractive, C=1-R, M=1-G, Y=1-B)
- HSL/HSV: intuitive editing (hue 0-360¬∞, saturation, lightness)
- XYZ: CIE standard, Y=luminance
- YUV: analog TV
- YCbCr: JPEG/MPEG (Y: 16-235, Cb/Cr: centered at 128)

flashcards.q: What is additive vs subtractive color?
flashcards.a: Additive (RGB): mixing light on screens. R+G=Yellow, R+G+B=White. Subtractive (CMY): mixing ink on paper. C+M+Y=Black. Start from white, add ink to remove color.

flashcards.q: What is the CMY formula and what does each ink absorb?
flashcards.a: C=1-R, M=1-G, Y=1-B (R,G,B in [0,1]). Cyan absorbs red. Magenta absorbs green. Yellow absorbs blue.

flashcards.q: Convert pure red (R=1,G=0,B=0) to CMY. Explain why.
flashcards.a: C=0, M=1, Y=1. Magenta ink removes green, Yellow ink removes blue вЖТ only red remains. No Cyan needed (cyan would remove red!).

flashcards.q: What are the hue angles for each color in HSL?
What do H, S, L mean?
flashcards.a: H=Hue (color type, 0-360¬∞). S=Saturation (vividness, 0=gray to 1=vivid). L=Lightness (0=black to 1=white).

flashcards.q: Why does YUV separate Y from UV? Two reasons.
flashcards.a: 1. Backwards compatibility: B&W TVs only show Y channel. 2. Compression: eye is less sensitive to color (UV) than brightness (Y), so UV can be compressed more.

flashcards.q: What are the YUV formulas and what do the weights mean?
flashcards.a: Y=0.299R+0.587G+0.114B. U=B-Y. V=R-Y. Green gets 0.587 (eye most sensitive). Blue gets 0.114 (eye least sensitive). Weights sum to 1.0 вЖТ gray gives Y=gray value.

flashcards.q: Convert pure blue (R=0, G=0, B=255) to YUV. Why is Y so low?
flashcards.a: Y=0.114√Ч255=29.07. U=225.93. V=вИТ29.07. Y is very low because the eye is least sensitive to blue (weight 0.114). Pure blue looks very dark to the human eye.

flashcards.q: In YCbCr (Rec. 601), what are the Y, Cb, Cr values for pure white? Pure black?
flashcards.a: White (R=G=B=1): Y=235, Cb=128, Cr=128. Black (R=G=B=0): Y=16, Cb=128, Cr=128. Black is Y=16 (NOT 0), white is Y=235 (NOT 255).

flashcards.q: What is chroma subsampling and why does it work?
flashcards.a: Reducing Cb and Cr resolution by half in each direction (keeping full Y resolution). Works because the eye is far less sensitive to color detail than brightness detail. Major reason JPEG achieves high compression.

problems.q: Convert R=200, G=100, B=50 to YUV. Interpret the sign of U and V.
problems.a:
$Y = 0.299 \times 200 + 0.587 \times 100 + 0.114 \times 50 = 59.8 + 58.7 + 5.7 = \mathbf{124.2}$

$U = 50 - 124.2 = \mathbf{-74.2}$ (negative = less blue than neutral gray)

$V = 200 - 124.2 = \mathbf{75.8}$ (positive = more red than neutral gray)

Makes sense: our pixel is reddish (R=200 >> B=50).

problems.q: Convert R=0, G=0, B=255 (pure blue) to YUV. Why is Y surprisingly low?
problems.a:
$Y = 0.299 \times 0 + 0.587 \times 0 + 0.114 \times 255 = \mathbf{29.07}$

$U = 255 - 29.07 = \mathbf{225.93}$

$V = 0 - 29.07 = \mathbf{-29.07}$

Y=29.07 is very low because **the eye is least sensitive to blue** (weight only 0.114). Pure blue appears nearly dark to the human visual system.
problems.hint: Blue weight in Y formula is 0.114 вАФ the smallest

problems.q: Convert R=0, G=255, B=0 (pure green) to YUV. Compare Y to pure blue.
problems.a:
$Y = 0.587 \times 255 = \mathbf{149.685}$

$U = 0 - 149.685 = \mathbf{-149.685}$

$V = 0 - 149.685 = \mathbf{-149.685}$

Y=149.685 vs Y=29.07 for blue. **Green appears ~5√Ч brighter than blue** to the eye вАФ this is why green dominates the Y formula.

problems.q: Convert R=1.0, G=0.0, B=0.0 (pure red) to CMY. Explain which inks are used and why.
problems.a:
$C = 1-1 = \mathbf{0}$, $M = 1-0 = \mathbf{1}$, $Y_c = 1-0 = \mathbf{1}$

Magenta ink + Yellow ink, no Cyan.
- Magenta absorbs green вЖТ removes green
- Yellow absorbs blue вЖТ removes blue
- What's left = red вЬУ

Cyan would absorb red вАФ exactly what we DON'T want.
problems.hint: C=1-R, M=1-G, Y=1-B. Think about what each ink absorbs.

problems.q: Convert R=1, G=0, B=0 (pure red) to YCbCr (Rec. 601). What do the Cb and Cr values tell you?
problems.a:
$Y = 65.481 \times 1 + 0 + 0 + 16 = \mathbf{81.5}$

$C_b = -37.797 \times 1 + 0 + 0 + 128 = \mathbf{90.2}$ (<128: less blue than neutral)

$C_r = 112 \times 1 + 0 + 0 + 128 = \mathbf{240}$ (maximum red!)

Y=81.5 is medium-dark (red isn't very bright to the eye). Cr=240 is near maximum (pure red).
problems.hint: Cb/Cr are centered at 128. Above 128 = more of that color.
